summary:
The French Revolution (1789–1799 AD) was the most transformative political upheaval of the modern era, dismantling the French ancien régime of absolute monarchy, aristocratic privilege, and clerical authority, and replacing it — through cascading phases of constitutional monarchy, radical republic, and Jacobin terror — with new principles of popular sovereignty, civic equality, and nationalism that permanently reshaped European and world politics. France in 1789 faced a fiscal crisis stemming from ruinous debts (partly incurred supporting the [[event:106|American Revolution]]), combined with bread shortages, aristocratic resistance to tax reform, and an educated middle class (bourgeoisie) inflamed by [[event:2346|Enlightenment]] ideas of natural rights and rational government. The convening of the [[event:2479|Estates General]] in May 1789 triggered a constitutional crisis: the Third Estate declared itself a National Assembly, the [[event:2353|Bastille was stormed]] in July, and the old regime collapsed with shocking speed.

The Revolution passed through several increasingly radical phases. The constitutional monarchy period (1789–1792) produced the [[event:2480|Declaration of the Rights of Man]], abolished feudalism, and nationalized church property. The declaration of the First French Republic (1792) and the [[event:2481|execution of Louis XVI]] (January 1793) horrified European monarchies and triggered the French Revolutionary Wars. The [[event:2354|Reign of Terror]] (1793–1794) under Robespierre and the Committee of Public Safety sent approximately 17,000 people to the guillotine in the name of republican virtue. Thermidorian reaction (1794) ended the Terror; the Directory (1795–1799) struggled with corruption and instability until [[event:2482|Napoleon Bonaparte's]] coup of 18 Brumaire (November 1799) ended the revolutionary decade. The Revolution's legacy — the Declaration of Rights, the abolition of feudalism, secularism, meritocracy, nationalism, and the very political vocabulary of left and right — proved permanently revolutionary for world history.
